# A360 Retrieval Architecture — Key Decisions

Architectural decisions that are expensive to retrofit. Lock these down before building. Companion to `RETRIEVAL_SERVICE.md` (the concrete spec).

**Status**: Approved
**Last Updated**: 2026-06-11

---

## 1. Citations must be born at retrieval time, never generated by the LLM

This is the single architectural decision everything else hangs on. The pipeline should be:

Retrieval returns chunks -> each chunk gets a stable retrieval ID (`src_1`, `src_2`...) carrying its full metadata (source type, source ID, URL, timestamp, snippet) -> the LLM sees only the chunk text plus its ID and is instructed to cite by ID -> your formatter maps IDs back to the metadata you already hold and builds the Citation objects deterministically.

The LLM never writes a PMID, a URL, a title, or an author. It only points at things you retrieved. This makes hallucinated citations structurally impossible rather than prompt-discouraged, and it makes validation trivial: any cited ID not in the retrieval set gets stripped and logged. If instead you let the model emit citation metadata, you'll spend months chasing fabricated DOIs. Build this as a small post-processing layer in your API, and it works identically for one-shot RAG answers and chat — same retrieval service, same formatter, different orchestration on top.

---

## 2. Capture deep-link offsets at ingestion — this is the biggest current gap

Clickable citations are only as good as the locator stored on the chunk. Looking at the v2 schemas:

- **PubMed**: fine. PMID -> `pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/{pmid}` works today.
- **YouTube**: `manufacturer_youtube_transcript` table has `chunk_index` but no `start_seconds`. Without per-chunk timestamps you can link to the video but not to the moment — and "watch this 25-minute video" is a much weaker citation than "jump to 14:32 where they demonstrate the injection plane." If the VTT/Whisper pipeline has timing data (it does — VTT is timestamped), persist `start_seconds`/`end_seconds` on every chunk now. Backfilling 202K chunks later means re-running ingestion.
- **Podcasts**: same problem, `podcast_chunks` has no timestamps. If the transcription source has them, store them; if not, accept episode-level links and don't promise more.
- **Manufacturer PDFs / FDA labels**: chunks need `page_number` captured at chunking time so the UI can open `{storage_url}#page=12`. Re-deriving page numbers from chunk text after the fact is miserable.

The rule: every chunk should carry enough metadata to construct its click target without any additional lookup. Audit that for each corpus before you ingest more, because ingestion-time is the only cheap time to capture it.

---

## 3. Cross-corpus score fusion is not free

When you fan out one query across four RPC functions, you get four lists of cosine similarities — and those numbers are not comparable across corpora. A 0.82 against a dense PubMed abstract and a 0.82 against rambling podcast speech don't mean the same thing. The authority weights (manufacturer 1.40, PubMed ~1.3, podcasts 0.90) are a reasonable static prior, but multiplying raw similarities by them will still produce skewed blends.

Two practical options: normalize per-corpus (z-score or min-max within each result list) before applying authority weights, or — better — over-retrieve (top 15-20 per corpus) and run a reranking pass over the combined pool. A cross-encoder reranker (Cohere Rerank, or Voyage's) or even a cheap LLM rerank call gives you a single comparable relevance score across all sources and dramatically improves what lands in context. Design the retrieval service with a rerank stage from day one even if you stub it initially; it's the highest-leverage quality knob in the whole system.

Also add hybrid search before you trust pure vector retrieval in this domain. Aesthetics is full of exact-match-critical terms — Daxxify, Sculptra, 510(k), "31-gauge" — that embedding similarity handles poorly. Postgres gives you `tsvector` full-text for free; run BM25-style and vector retrieval in parallel and fuse with reciprocal rank fusion. This is a few hours of SQL now versus a persistent "why didn't it find the obvious document" problem later.

---

## 4. Chat mode needs query rewriting, not just history-passing

The one-shot mode embeds the user's query directly. The chat mode cannot: "what about for men?" embeds to garbage without context. Standard pattern — a fast, cheap LLM call that condenses conversation history + new message into a standalone search query *before* embedding. Build the retrieval service to accept a rewritten query plus optional filters, and keep the rewriting step in the chat orchestrator. This also gives you a natural place to extract filters from conversation ("only peer-reviewed sources" -> corpus filter; patient in the room -> `patient_safe = true`).

For citation numbering in chat: number per-message, not per-conversation, but keep the underlying retrieval IDs globally stable so a user can ask "tell me more about source 2" and you can resolve it.

---

## 5. Decisions to make now, work to defer

**Embedding model**: ada-002 is dated, but you have 529K chunks on it and mixing models across corpora breaks unified search. Don't re-embed now. Do add a `model_version` column (or naming convention) to embedding storage so a future migration to text-embedding-3-large or Voyage can run corpus-by-corpus behind the abstraction. Locking yourself into "the embedding column is just ada-002 forever, implicitly" is the trap.

**Metadata pre-filtering**: you have GIN indexes on treatments/anatomy/concerns arrays — use them as pre-filters in the RPC functions (filter then rank), not post-filters on results, or filtered queries will return thin result sets. The `library_vocab` tagging is exactly the right asset here; the query rewriter can map user language onto vocab terms.

**Streaming**: a fan-out -> rerank -> generate pipeline will run 4-8 seconds. For a research chat that's tolerable only if tokens stream. Design the API as SSE from the start — stream the answer text, then emit citations as a final structured event. Retrofitting streaming into a request/response contract touches everything.

**Logging every retrieval set**: persist the full retrieved-chunk list (IDs + scores) per run, not just the final citations. When an answer is wrong, the first question is always "was it a retrieval failure or a generation failure," and you can't answer that without the retrieval log. This also becomes your eval substrate — a golden set of ~50 real practitioner questions with expected sources, scored on recall@k, is worth more than any amount of prompt tuning.

**Contract extension**: `CitationSourceType` still lacks `podcast` and `industry` — add them now along with timestamp fields in the metadata shapes, since the UI components key off source type for colors and link construction.

---

## 6. Confidence scores — what to show

The API contract asks LLMs to self-report 0-1 confidence and the UI renders "98% confident" next to clinical claims. Self-reported LLM confidence is decorative — it's not calibrated against anything. For retrieval results you can show similarity scores honestly; for generated claims, either define how the number is computed or drop the percentage from the UI. Showing fabricated precision on medical content is exactly the kind of thing a diligence reviewer flags.

**Decision**: confidence values come from retrieval scores (reranked or fused), not from the LLM. Display as a relevance indicator, not as "% confident."

---

## 7. Build order

1. Timestamp/page-number capture in ingestion (§2)
2. The retrieval-ID citation pipeline with validation (§1)
3. Hybrid retrieval + fusion in a single unified search endpoint (§3)
4. One-shot RAG answer end-to-end with rendered citations
5. The chat orchestrator with query rewriting on top of the same service (§4)
6. Reranking
7. Eval harness

Steps 1-2 are the ones you can't cheaply undo; everything after is iterative.
